coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of trace level Cd (II) triggered by chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) “turn on”: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as fluorometric paper-based sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Naksen, Puttaraksa
Boonruang, Siwaluck
Yuenyong, Nunthawan
Lee, Hooi Ling
Ramachandran, Pravena
Anutrasakda, Wipark
Amatatongchai, Maliwan
Pencharee, Somkid
Jarujamrus, Purim
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium ion (Cd (II)) is a highly toxic heavy metal usually found in natural water. Exposure to Cd (II) can produce serious effects in human organs such as Itai-Itai disease. Therefore, the maximum allowance levels of Cd (II) in drinking water and herbal medicines imposed by the World Health Organization (WHO) are 3 μg L−1 and 300 μg kg−1, respectively. In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a fluorescent sensor for Cd (II) determination was developed in both solution-based and paper-based systems. N-GQDs were synthesized from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) via the hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-GQDs emitted intense blue fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of up to 80%. The functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs measured by FTIR were carboxyl (COO−), hydroxyl (OH−), and amine (NH2) groups, suggesting that they could be bound to Cd (II) for complexation. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was gradually enhanced with the increase of Cd (II) concentration. This phenomenon was proved to result from the fluorescence enhancement (turn-on) based on the chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. Under the optimum conditions in the solution-based and paper-based systems, the limits of detection (LODs) were found to be 1.09 and 0.59 μg L−1, respectively. Furthermore, the developed sensors showed relatively high selectivity toward Cd (II) over ten other metal cations and six other anions of different charges. The performance of the sensor in real water and herbal medicine samples exhibited no significant difference as compared to the results of the validation method (ICP-OES). Therefore, the developed sensors can be used as fluorescent sensors for Cd (II) determination with high sensitivity, high selectivity, short incubation time (5 min). As such, the paper-based strategy has excellent promising potential for practical analysis of Cd (II) in water and herbal medicine samples with a trace level of Cd (II) concentrations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cadmium ion (Cd (II))
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Fluorescent sensor
Paper-based sensor
Chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the fluorometric paper-based sensor for Cd (II) determination
Selectivity of the fluorometric paper-based sensor for Cd (II)
Determination of Cd (II) in real samples
Optimization of sensor parameters for the solution-based system
N-GQDs sensor for Cd (II) determination in the solution-based
Optimization of sensor parameters for the paper-based system
N-GQDs sensor for Cd(II) determination in the paper-based systems
Selectivity of the developed paper-based sensor for Cd (II) determination
N-GQDs-based sensor for cadmium determination in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are gratefully acknowledged. This work is supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (IRN/502/2563) chaired by Assoc. Prof. Dr Duangjai Nacapricha. The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation is gratefully acknowledged. Prof. Dr. Vinich Promarak, Vidyasirimedhi institute of science and technology (VISTEC) are gratefully also acknowledged for the fluorescence lifetime characterization. The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are also gratefully acknowledged. Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) given to Puttaraksa Naksen is also gratefully acknowledged.

The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are gratefully acknowledged. This work is supported by the National Research Council of Thailand ( IRN/502/2563 ) chaired by Assoc. Prof. Dr Duangjai Nacapricha. The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation is gratefully acknowledged. Prof. Dr. Vinich Promarak, Vidyasirimedhi institute of science and technology (VISTEC) are gratefully also acknowledged for the fluorescence lifetime characterization. The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are also gratefully acknowledged. Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) given to Puttaraksa Naksen is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: size distribution) and (b) FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of N-GQDs (inset; photos of N-GQDs under daylight (left) and a UV lamp at 365 nm (right)) and (d) zeta potentials of N-GQDs in different pH solutions (n = 3).

Fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs sensor on the solution-based system at (a) different pH values and (b) different incubation. (c) Fluorescence enhancement of the N-GQDs-Cd (II) system containing different concentrations of Cd (II) in the solution-based system (inset: calibration curve of I–I0 versus concentration of Cd (II)).

(a) TEM image of N-GQDs-Cd (II); (b) zeta potentials of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II); (c) energy diagram of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II); (d) the survey XPS spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II); (e and f) the high resolution XPS spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II), respectively.

The fluorescence efficiency of N-GQDs sensor on the paper-based system at (a) different concentrations of Cd (ll) and (b) different incubation times. Linearity range of the enhancement efficiency of (c) the paper-based system.

(a) Preparation of fluorescent N-GQDs; (b) turn-on fluorescence of N-GQDs based on the CHEF mechanism; (c and d) Fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs in solution-based and paper-based systems, respectively.
Effect of ion interference on Cd (II) determination using the developed paper-based system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tolerance ratio was defined as the maximum concentration of each interference ion which provides %relative error less than ±5%; control: the grey intensity in blue channel of 2.5 μg L−1 Cd (II) 219.69 ± 0.75. Test ion: the grey intensity in blue channel of 2.5 μg L−1 of Cd (II) standard is plus interference ion.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculation of %relative error: %relative error = {[average the grey intensity in blue channel of N-GQDs with Cd(II) and interference − average the grey intensity in blue channel of N-GQDs with Cd(II)]/average the grey intensity in blue channel of (N-GQDs with Cd(II))} × 100.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Cd (II) in real samples using turn-on fluorescent probes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND: not detectable (the intensity of fluorescence cannot be detectable), —: not applicable.

*Calculation of % relative error: % relative error = ([found concentration obtained from paper or solution-based system - found concentration obtained from ICP-OES]/found concentration obtained from ICP-OES) × 100.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Puttaraksa Naksen and Siwaluck Boonruang equally as co-first authors.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of trace level Cd (II) triggered by chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) “turn on”: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as fluorometric paper-based sensor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium ion (Cd (II)) is a highly toxic heavy metal usually found in natural water. Exposure to Cd (II) can produce serious effects in human organs such as Itai-Itai disease. Therefore, the maximum allowance levels of Cd (II) in drinking water and herbal medicines imposed by the World Health Organization (WHO) are 3 μg L−1 and 300 μg kg−1, respectively. In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a fluorescent sensor for Cd (II) determination was developed in both solution-based and paper-based systems. N-GQDs were synthesized from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) via the hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-GQDs emitted intense blue fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of up to 80%. The functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs measured by FTIR were carboxyl (COO−), hydroxyl (OH−), and amine (NH2) groups, suggesting that they could be bound to Cd (II) for complexation. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was gradually enhanced with the increase of Cd (II) concentration. This phenomenon was proved to result from the fluorescence enhancement (turn-on) based on the chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. Under the optimum conditions in the solution-based and paper-based systems, the limits of detection (LODs) were found to be 1.09 and 0.59 μg L−1, respectively. Furthermore, the developed sensors showed relatively high selectivity toward Cd (II) over ten other metal cations and six other anions of different charges. The performance of the sensor in real water and herbal medicine samples exhibited no significant difference as compared to the results of the validation method (ICP-OES). Therefore, the developed sensors can be used as fluorescent sensors for Cd (II) determination with high sensitivity, high selectivity, short incubation time (5 min). As such, the paper-based strategy has excellent promising potential for practical analysis of Cd (II) in water and herbal medicine samples with a trace level of Cd (II) concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental contamination by heavy metals has become one of the major global problems. Among other heavy metals, cadmium ion (Cd (II)) is known for being highly and has threatened the health of human beings and the ecosystem worldwide. Cd (ll) is widely used in many applications such as the weapons industry, metallurgy electroplating and agriculture [1]. Even a minute amount of Cd (ll) can cause harmful exposure to humans, often through the consumption of polluted food or water. The exposure of Cd (II) can produce serious effects in human organs such as Itai-Itai disease [2]. The maximum allowance levels of Cd (II) in drinking water and herbal medicines imposed by the World Health Organization (WHO) are 3 μg L−1 and 300 μg kg−1, respectively [3,4]. Therefore, sensitive determination of Cd (ll) is highly desired. Well-known detection approaches for Cd (ll) ions include atomic absorption spectrometry [5] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [6]. Although these methods are highly selective and sensitive to Cd (II), the complicated procedures for sample preparation and the high cost as well as time-consuming detection process prohibit many practical applications.

Recent attempts to develop sensitive and selective Cd (II) quantification have focused much interest on fluorescent methods due to their high selectivity and sensitivity and low cost. Among other fluorescent materials, quantum dots (QDs) have gained significant attention since they offer several advantages including high sensitivity, high photostability, strong resistance and biocompatibility. QDs can be classified into two types: transition metal-based dots (e.g., CdTe, CdSe, and CdS) and carbon-based dots (CDs) [7]. Among others, CDs have shown particularly high potential for sensitive and selective detection of Cd (II) owing to the several significant advantages over transition metal-based dots. Particularly, the synthesis of CDs requires no heavy metals and therefore, has low toxicity. In addition, the advantages of CDs also include low cost, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and simple synthetic routes [8].

To further improve quantum yield (QY), fluorescent properties and other physicochemical properties of CDs and therefore, expand their application scope, CDs can be doped with heteroatoms [9]. Among others, doping CDs with nitrogen can improve the emission properties of CDs by contributing unpaired electrons of nitrogen to CDs. The improved properties are likely to further endow nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a greater potential for use as an effective dual sensor or probe. It is to be noted that for the use of CDs as a fluorescent sensor, the fundamental mechanism should also be investigated especially in the areas of CDs structure and their heteroatom function type to bind with analyte [10,11].

CDs can be divided into three types including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). Many research groups have developed N-CDs fluorescent sensors for the determination of Cd (II) based on turn-off methodologies (static quenching mechanism). For instance, Niu. et al. [12] synthesized carbon quantum dots/gold nanoclusters (CQDs/AuNCs) nanohybrid based ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cd (II) determination. They proposed CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid which exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity toward Cd (II) with a detection limit of 3.65 μg L−1. The mechanism was based on the static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cd (II) to CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. Gu et al. [13] synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using scallion (SL) as the carbon source. The fluorescent intensity of this probe decreased linearly with increasing concentration of Cd (II) in the range of 11.24–337.2 and 562–3372 μg L−1. The detection limit of Cd (II) was found to be 1.69 μg L−1. This N,S-CDs probe was applied to determine Cd (II) in real water and serum samples in a solution system based on a static quenching mechanism.

Efforts incorporating the use of N-CDs fluorescent sensors for the determination of Cd (II) also include those of Yang et al. [14], where N-CQDs were synthesized with a high QY of 80.4%. The quenching fluorescent intensity was linearly related in the range of 20–300 μg L−1 with the LOD of 20.69 μg L−1. However, their investigation did not involve real samples. Additionally, Tang et al. [15] developed a Cd (II) sensor using nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with 2-mercaptothiazoline (2-MT) and triethylenetetramine (TETA) as precursors. The obtained N,S-CDs exhibited bright blue emission with a QY of 40% and have a fast responsive fluorescence quenching signal within 2 min via static quenching mechanism. The fluorescent intensity of this probe decreased linearly with increasing the concentration of Cd (II) in the range of 22.48–112.4 μg L−1 with the detection limit of 2.02 μg L−1.

The aforementioned N-CDs fluorescent sensors were based on turn-off methodologies. To the best of our knowledge, there is only one work reporting the use of N-CDs fluorescent sensors for the determination of Cd (II) based on turn-on methodologies (chelation enhanced fluorescence; CHEF). Particularly, Lin et al. [16] synthesized nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) via hydrothermal method using o-phosphorylethanolamine and citric acid as precursors. The blue-green fluorescence of the N,P-CDs (with excitation/emission peaks at 325/435 nm) gradually enhanced upon sequential addition of Cd (II). This fluorometric assay worked in the 56.2–1405 μg L−1 Cd (II) concentration range with a detection limit of 17.98 μg L−1. The mechanism could be attributed to chelation enhanced fluorescence that was induced by the formation of Cd (II)–N,P-CDs complex with functional groups present on the surface. The method was applied for Cd (II) determination in spiked serum and urine samples.

So far, the development of carbon nanodots for the application of Cd (II) determination has largely involved solution-based systems. Meanwhile, the Cd (II) determination in paper-based systems has not been reported. Furthermore, the majority of the reported CDs probes for Cd (II) determination exhibited low QY. The insights into the mechanism of the Cd (II) determination are also still in need of further investigation. It is therefore the aim of this work to address these limitations.

This work proposes a novel and simple method of solution-based and paper-based sensors for the sensitive determination of Cd (II) at trace level using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a fluorescent sensor. N-GQDs were synthesized from citric acid (as a carbon source) and ethylenediamine (as a nitrogen source) through a hydrothermal synthesis process [17]. To achieve high sensitivity and selectivity for Cd (II) determination in real samples, the parameters affecting the performance of the sensors including the concentration of N-GQDs, pH of the system, and incubation time, were optimized. For the paper-based sensor, the optimization was included color channels, concentration of N-GQDs, volume of reagent mixing, and incubation time. In addition, stability and the effect of ion interferences were investigated for the developed sensors. The developed sensors were applied to real water and herbal medicine samples including drinking water, mineral drinking water, tap water, ginger rhizome, and galangal rhizome. The mechanism of the use of N-GQDs for Cd (II) determination was also investigated.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Cadmium nitrate was purchased from Carlo Erba (Milan, Italy). Citric acid-1-hydrate (Bendosen, Laboratory Chemicals, Malaysia) and ethylenediamine (QREC, Grade AR, (Asia) Sdn. Bhd, Malaysia) were employed in the preparation of N-GQDS. Sodium diphosphate and disodium phosphate were purchased from Biochemical (Shah Alam, Malaysia). Whatman No. 1 filter paper was purchased from Whatman International Ltd. (Maidstone, England). Double-sided adhesive tape was obtained from a local stationary store (Ubon Ratchathani, Thailand). Deionized water with 18.2 MΩ resistance from a Millipore Milli-Q purification system (Bedford, USA) was used in all experiments.

The morphology of N-GQDs was investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 (URP), JEOL, Japan) with 200-mesh Cu grids coated with carbon film and scanning electron microscope coupled with energy dispersive spectrometer (SEM-EDS, JSM-IT300, JEOL, Japan) using a platinum film coated on the specimen. Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZS, Malvern, UK) was used to determine the zeta potentials of N-GQDs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS, Nano ZS, Malvern, UK) was used to determine hydrodynamic sizes of N-GQDs. Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA) was recorded with attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 for the identification of functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos Analytical, Manchester, U.K.) was used for the investigation of elemental composition and elemental state in N-GQDs. The photoluminescent emission of the samples was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (model LS55; PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, USA). The absorption spectra of N-CDs were collected using a double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) within a spectral range of 200–800 nm to study the molecular electronic transitions. The time-resolved fluorescence lifetime was measured with a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) device. A picosecond diode laser (Nanoled, IBH, UK) was used as the light source at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The signal was detected at the magic angle (54.7°) polarization using a Hamamastsu MCP PMT (3809U) and the decays were analyzed using IBH DAS-6 decay analysis software. Absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured using an integrating sphere with a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980 Edinburgh Instruments, UK). A camera attached to a smartphone (Galaxy A50, Samsung, South Korea) was used as a colorimetric analyzer.

N-GQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method based on a previous study [17], in which citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were used as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. In a 5 mL ultrapure water solution, 1 mol of CA and 1 mol of EDA were dissolved and agitated until a clear solution was formed. The solution was then placed in a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated for 4 h at 180 °C. The obtained black-brown transparent solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the product was dialyzed in 1 kDa dialysis bag for 24 h to remove the unreacted chemicals. Finally, the red-brown transparent solution was collected and stored in the dark at 4 °C for future use. The stability of the synthesized N-GQDs analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer was found to last for more than 9 months.

The approximate concentration of N-GQDs was calculated using the following steps. First, the clear glass slide was weighed and recorded. Then, the original N-GQDs were accurately dropped onto the glass slide and dried in an oven at 70 °C until the solvent completely evaporated (approximately 16 h). Finally, the residues on the glass slide were weighed and compared to that of the bare glass slide. The calculated concentration of N-GQDs was then obtained (Schematic diagram a).

50 μL of N-GQDs stock solution (16.32 mg L−1) was dissolved in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6). Then, 50 μL of Cd (II) solution at each concentration ranging from 0.01 to 10 μg L−1 was added to the above solution, followed by the addition of 1400 μL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6). The mixture was then incubated for 5 min to equilibrate. The fluorescence intensity was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer and reported as fluorescence enhancement (I–I0, where I and I0 are fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs in the presence and absence of Cd (II), respectively). The excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength used were 340 and 440 nm, respectively (Schematic diagram c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the fluorometric paper-based sensor for Cd (II) determination
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorometric paper-based sensor for Cd (II) determination was fabricated by attaching two pieces of Whatman No. 1 filter paper using double-sided adhesive tape, followed by cutting with a hole puncher into a circular shape with 6 mm diameter. For the determination of Cd (II) concentration, 3 μL of N-GQDs 3400 mg L−1 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6) was dropped onto the paper-based sensor and let to dry at room temperature. Then, 5 μL of each Cd (II)-containing sample was dropped onto the paper-based sensor. These paper-based sensors were allowed to stand for 5 min to equilibrate. Pictures were recorded using a smartphone camera in a controlled light box (rectangular shape, with the dimension of 36 by 47 by 24 cm) equipped with a 365 nm UV lamp on each side. The color intensity was further determined using ImageJ software based on the grey intensity in the blue channel (Schematic diagram d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fluorometric paper-based sensor for Cd (II)
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the developed sensor for Cd (II) determination, the following procedure was carried out to examine the effect of coexisting ions. Briefly, 3 μL of N-GQDs (3400 mg L−1) in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6) was dropped onto the paper-based sensor and the sensor was allowed to stand at room temperature until dry. Then, 5 μL of each mixed solution of 2.5 μg L−1 Cd (II) and one of the following interferences: As (III), Hg (II), Mg (II), Ni (II), Zn (II), Fe (III), Cu (II), Ba (II), Ag (I), oxide, chloride, sulfate, and nitrate was dropped onto the paper-based device. These paper-based sensors were allowed to stand for 5 min to equilibrate. Pictures of the paper-based sensors were recorded using a smartphone camera in a controlled light box equipped with a 365 nm UV lamp on each side. The color intensity was further determined using ImageJ software based on the grey intensity in the blue channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd (II) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real water samples including drinking water, mineral drinking water, and tap water were used for practical applications. The drinking water and mineral drinking water were purchased from a convenient store in Ubon Ratchathani, Thailand. The tap water was obtained from a source at Ubon Ratchathani University. All water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm-syringe filter before use.

Ginger and galangal rhizomes purchased from a local market in Ubon Ratchathani, Thailand were used as representative herbal samples. The rhizomes of ginger and galangal were cleaned, cut into small pieces, dried, and grind into powder. Then, 0.5 g of the powder was mixed with 10 mL of deionized water. Then the precipitate was filtrate through Whatman No. 4 filter paper [18].

Each solution of the as-prepared water and herbal samples was then spiked with different concentrations of standard Cd (II) solution and analyzed using a fluorescence spectrometer based on the solution system. For the paper-based system, the as-prepared samples were accurately dropped onto the paper-based sensor before the analysis.

In this study, N-GQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal process and characterized for their physical properties (by TEM, AFM, FTIR, XPS, DLS, and zeta potential techniques) and optical properties (by UV–vis and fluorescence techniques). The TEM image of N-GQDs shows that N-GQDs particles were spherical in shape and exhibited a homogeneous distribution (Fig. 1 a). The size distribution of the particles was estimated using ImageJ software and graphically represented in the inset of Fig. 1a. The average particle diameter (calculated from 80 random particles) of N-GQDs was found to be 3.2 ± 1.5 nm. The cross-sectional view of N-GQDs obtained from the AFM technique shows that the majority of topographic heights of N-GQDs were less than 1 nm, which was an evident illustration of a single-layer of N-GQDs (Fig. S1). The result corresponds well with our previous HRTEM of N-GQDs, confirming the crystallinity of N-GQDs [17]. The average hydrodynamic particles size measured by the DLS technique was found to be 232.3 ± 63.0 nm. The larger average size of N-GQDs obtained from the DLS technique as compared to the TEM technique is attributed to the fact that the surface of N-GQDs in the solution was surrounded by hydrophilic functional groups. Specifically, the surface of N-GQDs consisted of hydrophilic functional groups that could be extended when N-GQDs were dispersed in deionized water. This surface characteristic is usually caused by incomplete carbonization of polymer chains (after dehydration of dissolved ethylenediamine as a precursor) during the bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis of N-GQDs and the step of dialysis, leading to a large hydrodynamic diameter [12,13]. The zeta potential of N-GQDs measured by zeta-sizer was found to be −15.8 ± 0.2 mV in the deionized water medium, indicating the presence of negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of N-GQDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, N-GQDs exhibited broad –OH, –NH2, and –CH stretching peaks at ca. 3231, 3074 and 2904 cm−1, respectively. Additional peaks due to -C-O, –CC, -C-N, and –CN stretches were also found at ca. 1648, 1531, 1385, and 1220 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, the result is in agreement with Lee et al. report [17]. Overall, the FT-IR result suggests that the surface of N-GQDs was enriched with hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amine (-NH2) functional groups, corresponding to the XPS results (Fig. 3d) and e) and S2). Especially, the XPS result are correspond to previous report [19–21].

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 1c) shows strong absorption peaks at around 240 nm and 347 nm. The peak at 240 nm is due to the transition from π to π* of CC and CN bonds while the peak at 347 nm is due to the transition from n to π* of CO and C–N bonds in heterocyclic aromatic moieties of N-GQDs. The presence of carboxyl and carbonyl functional groups were likely originated from the surface of N-GQDs and the CC groups from the sp2 hybridized polymeric domains in the core. On the other hand, the presence of the C–N functional groups was from ethylenediamine as the source of N atom. The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs (567 μg L−1) using different excitation wavelengths (λ ex) ranging from 310 nm to 400 nm are shown in Fig. S3. The results show that when λ ex was increased from 310 to 340 nm, the fluorescence intensity also increased and the maximum emission wavelength (λ em) around 440 nm was observed with bright blue photoluminescence for the λ ex of 340 nm. However, when λ ex was further increased up to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity dramatically decreased but λ em was still observed at 440 nm (Fig. 1c). It should be noted that the electron-donating amine groups have a better ability to donate electrons as compared to the hydroxyl groups and therefore, can further enhance the luminescence of N-GQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (absolute QY) and the fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs were determined to be 80% and 15.0 ns, respectively. Our result corresponds well with the absolute QY of N-GQDs using critic acid and ethylene diamine as the sources for carbon and nitrogen, respectively [22]. The morphology, functional groups and optical properties results of N-GQDs are in agreement with a previous report [17].

The isoelectric point (pI) and stability of N-GQDs were investigated by monitoring their charge and fluorescence intensity over various pH values and storage times, respectively. Specifically, the effect of pH solution in the range of 1.0–12.0 was investigated by adjusting the pH solution using 0.1 M of HCl acid or 0.1 M of NaOH solution. The results show that the zeta potential of N-GQDs approached 0 mV for the N-GQDs solution at pH 3.0 (Fig. 1d), which indicate that the pI value of N-GQDs is ∼3.0. Additionally, the results also show that the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was high for solutions with pH values between 5.0 and 11.0, which likely resulted from the deprotonation process of –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 functional groups (Fig. S4a). Storage N-GQDs under 4 °C with light-protected conditions in a refrigerator. Result from stability study reveals that the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs did not significantly change over 9 months and no precipitated or floating particles were observed (Fig. S4b). These findings suggest the high stability and long shelf-life of the synthesized N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensor parameters for the solution-based system
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize conditions for Cd (II) determination using N-GQDs solution-based sensor, concentration of N-GQDs, pH, and incubation time were investigated. First, the concentration of N-GQDs was optimized and it was found that the fluorescence intensity of pure N-GQDs was directly proportional to the concentration of N-GQDs in the range of 42.5–680.0 μg L−1 (Fig. S5). In spite of the directly proportional relationship, the highest concentration (680.0 μg L−1) of pure N-GQDs, which gave the highest fluorescence intensity, was not used. Specifically, choosing the highest concentration may not be appropriate since room for further increase of fluorescence intensity must be provided. Particularly, a lower concentration, and therefore, a lower fluorescence intensity, are required to allow the effect of the reaction between N-GQDs and Cd (II) for the “turn-on” fluorescence sensing system (vide infra). On the other hand, extremely low concentrations of N-GQDs were also not appropriate since the concentration might not be sufficient to complete the reaction with Cd (II). Therefore, the concentration of N-GQDs of 544 μg L−1 (red line, Fig. S5) was selected for further studies.

In the case of the optimization of pH values, those in the range of 4–12 (where high tolerance of N-GQDs was observed; Fig. S4 a) were investigated. At each pH value, four Cd (II) concentrations (0, 1, 10, and 20 μg L−1) in the N-GQDs-Cd (II) system were investigated for the highest selectivity of the detection system. The term I–I0 was used to represent the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs (Fig. 2 a). As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence intensity measurements across the N-GQDs-Cd (II) samples were significantly different, and the enhancing process between N-GQDs and Cd (II) was most highly efficient at pH 6. Therefore, pH 6 was selected for further studies. It is to be noted that the interaction between N-GQDs and Cd (II) was based on the hard/soft-acid/base (HSAB) theory, which will be discussed later in the study of selectivity section [23,24].

Finally, the effect of incubation time from 3 to 40 min was investigated (Fig. 2b). The results show that the fluorescence enhancement of the N-GQDs-Cd (II) system was relatively stable over the incubation time of 3–40 min. This finding suggests that the developed sensor is promising for rapid sensing of Cd (II) where a strict time control is not required. To ensure the equilibration was reached, the incubation time of 5 min was selected for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GQDs sensor for Cd (II) determination in the solution-based
ce:section.ce:para: For the solution-based system, the fluorescence enhancement of the N-GQDs-Cd (II) system measured at 440 nm under the optimum conditions is shown in Fig. 2c and S6. Specifically, I–I0 (where I and I0 are fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of Cd (II), respectively) are represented against different concentrations of Cd (II) ranging from 0.5 to 50.0 μg L−1. The results indicate that, for the Cd (II) concentration range of 1.0–25.0 μg L−1, the fluorescence enhancement of N-GQDs-Cd (II) linearly increased with the increase of Cd (II) concentration (r2 = 0.9942; insert Fig. 2c). The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 1.09 μg L−1 (calculated from 3SDblank/slope; n = 10). The precision for the determination of Cd (II) concentration using the developed method was also investigated by performing intra-day and inter-day experiments (Table S2). The results also show that the % RSD of intra-day and inter-day was not higher than 2.67% and 2.81%, respectively.

The origin and mechanism for photoluminescence of N-GQDs are still topics to be further explored. Nonetheless, it is widely accepted that the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs arises from the radioactive combination of the trapped surface holes and electrons on their surface [25]. Based on the HSAB theory, Cd (II), known as a “soft acid”, preferentially chelate with “soft base” groups on the surface of N-GQDs. Especially, the nitrogen atoms of N-GQDs can coordinate to Cd (II) to form a stable N-GQDs -Cd (II) complex by sharing lone pairs of electrons to empty orbitals of Cd (II) [26]. This occurrence leads to an increase in fluorescence intensity with increasing Cd (II) concentration.

Using N-GQDs as a fluorescence sensor for Cd (II) determination by the turn-on mechanism, the detection concept is based on the functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine) on the surface of N-GQDs. These functional groups interacted selectively with Cd (II) depending on the pH of the detection system. Specifically, the XPS signals due to the graphitic-N, pyridinic-N and oxidized N moieties dramatically decreased (Fig. 3 e–f), suggesting that Cd (II) favorably formed complexes to these N moieties.

The aforementioned turn-on phenomenon is believed to result from the chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. The principle of this phenomenon is the chelation of N-GQDs and Cd (II), leading to the inhibition of photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and hence, enhancing the radiative decay pathway [27,28]. This explanation is supported by complexation evidence such as the energy level of the electron, morphology, and binding energy. The energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO) of N-GQDs were estimated by using the following empirical equations (Eqs. (1)–(3)) [9,29].
(1)
E HOMO
=
− e
(
E ox
+
4.4
)
(2)
E LUMO
=
− e
(
E red
+
4.4
)
(3)
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
−
E g
where Eox and Ered are the onsets of oxidation and reduction potentials of N-GQDs, respectively, and Eg is the energy bandgap. The Ered values of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II) measured by cyclic voltammetry were found to be −0.363 V and −0.344 V (with reference to Ag/AgCl) and −0.166 V and −0.147 V (with reference to SHE), respectively (Fig. S8a-b). The Eg values of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II) estimated from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum were found to be 3.11 and 3.14 eV, respectively (Fig. S8c-d). Based on these experimental values, the ELUMO and EHOMO of N-GQDs were then calculated to be −4.234 eV and −7.344 eV, respectively. Also, the ELUMO and EHOMO of N-GQDs-Cd (II) were calculated to be −4.254 eV and −7.393 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c)).

Based on the CHEF process, the stable N-GQDs-Cd (II) complex was formed after the chelation between N-GQDs and Cd (II). The formation of N-GQDs-Cd (II) complex is evidenced by the decrease in the EHOMO value for the formation of N-GQDs-Cd (II) complex (Schematic diagram b). Furthermore, the results obtained from TEM, DLS, zeta potential, and XPS techniques confirm the formation of NGQDs-Cd(II) at pH 6. Specifically, the particle sizes of N-GQDs-Cd (II) obtained from TEM analysis (Fig. 3a) were significantly larger than those of pure N-GQDs (7.2 ± 2.1 nm vs 3.2 ± 1.5 nm) (Fig. 1a) This finding is indicative of the complex formation of Cd (II) with the surface function groups of N-GQDs. The average hydrodynamic particles size of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II) were found to be 228.2 ± 17 and 431.75 ± 50 nm (Table S1), respectively.

Furthermore, the zeta potential of N-GQDs-Cd (II) was significantly less negative than that of N-GQDs (−4.64 mV) (Fig. 3b). This finding confirms the binding of Cd (II) with –NH2 groups on the surface of N-GQDs. The XPS spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Cd (II) indicate the presence of C, N, and O atoms (Fig. 3d). For the case of N-GQDs-Cd (II), the peak due to Cd (II) was also observed. Additionally, the high resolution XPS spectra of N 1s show that the signals due to the graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N (most found) and oxidized N dramatically decreased in N-GQDs-Cd (II) when compared to an original signal of N-GQDs without Cd (II) (Fig. 3e–f).

Overall, the driving force for the CHEF process was the suitable energy gap between the LUMO of N-GQDs (anion radical formed) and the LUMO of Cd (II) (cation radical formed) as shown in Fig. 3c. This phenomenon led to the ionic interaction between Cd (II) with the hydroxy, carboxylic, and amine groups on the surface of N-GQDs. Therefore, the fluorescence enhancement is likely the result of inhibition of photo-induced electron transfer [30] assigned to the formation of N-GQDs-Cd (II) complexes. In other words, CHEF is likely the main mechanism for the fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensor parameters for the paper-based system
ce:section.ce:para: First, the overall design of the paper-based device in this work was studied. The device was fabricated according to our previous report [10], consisting of two pieces of filter paper attached to each other with double-sided adhesive tape. The fabricated device offers many advantages. Specifically, the double-sided adhesive tape underneath the filter paper acts as a reservoir that can retain a significant number of particles of N-GQDs tethered on filter paper. This characteristic helps reduce the reflection of the background from the adhesive tape during the picture recording using a digital camera under a control light box and therefore further improves the performance of the detection zone [10]. Hence, this optimized design was chosen for the fabrication of the paper-based device as Cd (II) sensor in this work.

Next, an appropriate interpretation of the color on the paper-based sensor for Cd (II) determination was investigated. Specifically, the grey intensity (in red, green, and blue) of N-GQDs-Cd (II) with different Cd (II) concentrations was plotted against the concentration of Cd (II) (Fig. S7a). The results show that the grey intensity in the blue channel was significantly higher than that of other channels at all concentrations of Cd (II) investigated. This finding is as expected since the fluorescence emission of the N-GQDs used in this work was blue [31]. Therefore, the grey intensity in the blue channel was selected for Cd (II) determination in the paper-based system.

To optimize the volume of reagent mixing, 1, 3, and 5 μL of both N-GQDs and Cd (II) were investigated at the fixed concentrations of N-GQDs at 3400 mg L−1 and Cd (II) at 10 μg L−1. The response was assessed in terms of the enhancement efficiency (B–B0), where B0 is the grey intensity in the blue channel in the absence of Cd (II) and B is the grey intensity in the blue channel in the presence of Cd (II). The effect of the volume of N-GQDs was shown in Fig. S7b. After letting stand at room temperature for 30 min for drying, the use of 3 μL of N-GQDs solution showed the highest enhancement efficiency while that of 5 μL exhibited the lowest enhancement efficiency. The difference in enhancement efficiency was likely due to the reflection of moist from the N-GQDs solution on the paper-based device [10]. Furthermore, the use of 5 μL of Cd (II) solution was found to exhibit the highest enhancement efficiency (Fig. S7c). Therefore, 3 μL and 5 μL were selected as the volumes of N-GQDs and Cd (II) for further studies, respectively.

To optimize the concentration of N-GQDs, the stock N-GQDs solution (84,000 mg L−1) was diluted into three concentrations: 1,133, 3400 and 8500 mg L−1 at pH 6 (Fig. 4 a). The results show that in the presence of 0.5, 1.0, 2.5 and 5.0 μg L−1 of Cd (II), the values of B–B0 increased with increasing Cd (II) concentration in most cases. Nevertheless, the use of 3400 mg L−1 N-GQDs exhibited the strongest linear relationship and therefore was selected for further studies.

The effect of incubation time from 1 to 10 min was also investigated (Fig. 4b). The results show that the enhancement efficiency of the N-GQDs-Cd (II) system increased from 1 to 5 min and remained quite flat afterward. Therefore, 5 min of incubation time was selected for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GQDs sensor for Cd(II) determination in the paper-based systems
ce:section.ce:para: For the paper-based system, the grey intensity in the blue channel (B) of N-GQDs on paper-based device under the optimum conditions decreased with increasing Cd (II) concentration. The plot of Cd (II) concentration against the B–B0 value is shown in Fig. 4c. The result shows good linearity in the Cd (II) concentration range of 1.0–15.0 μg L−1 with r2 = 0.9951. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 0.59 μg L−1 (calculated from 3SDblank/slope; n = 10). The % RSD of intra-day and inter-day experiments was not higher than 5.91% and 0.23%, respectively. These results indicate that the developed method is sensitive to Cd (II) at low concentration levels, which is suitable for real sample applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the developed paper-based sensor for Cd (II) determination
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is among the most important factors affecting the efficiency of Cd (II) determination approach. Therefore, under the optimal conditions, the selectivity of N-GQDs toward Cd (II) was investigated in the presence of the following common interference ions: As (III), Hg (II), Mg (II), Ni (II), Zn (II), Fe (III), Cu (II), Ba (II), Ag (I), oxide, chloride, sulfate, and nitrate (Table 1 ). The tolerance ratio is defined as the highest concentration of each interference that can be added to the N-GQDs-Cd (II) system (with 2.5 μg L−1 Cd (II) concentration for the paper-based system) and still maintain the grey intensity in the blue channel within a 5% relative error between the experiment with and without any interference.

The results show that the N-GQDs sensor had high tolerance toward Hg(II), Mg(II), Cu(II), Ag(I), Pb(II), sulfate, and nitrate with a tolerance ratio over 1000. For Cl- and oxide were found to be 353.51 and 82.03, respectively. The tolerance ratio of 50 was found for As(III), Fe(III), SO3 2−, and S2− For the others, the tolerance ratio of 10 was found (Table 1). The main reason for high selectivity for the determination of Cd (II) over the interference ions could be explained by the HSAB theory as mentioned earlier. Particularly, the majority of covalent or coordinative interactions with nitrogen-containing species on the surface of N-GQDs involved Cd (II), which is a soft cation. This phenomenon was driven by the fact that, as evidenced by the XPS results, Cd (II) ions have higher affinity and were more stable than the other interference metal ions at pH 6 [9,24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GQDs-based sensor for cadmium determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed solution-based and paper-based sensors were further investigated for the determination of Cd (II) in real samples including drinking water, mineral drinking water, tap water, ginger, and galangal rhizome (Table 2 ). Each sample was spiked with 1.00–25.00 μg L−1 of standard Cd (II) solution. The results indicate that Cd (II) was undetectable in all unspiked samples as measured with both solution-based and paper-based sensors. In the spiked samples, the recovery was found to be in the ranges of 91.40–106.60% and 92.40–107.00% for the solution-based sensor and paper-based sensor, respectively. The results obtained from the developed methods were also compared to those obtained from ICP-OES as the validation method. The comparison showed no significant difference between the two methods. In particular, the t stat value of 0.297 was lower than the t critical value of 2.086 for the solution-based system, and the t stat value of 0.183 was lower than the t critical value of 2.048 for the paper-based system. These findings suggest that the developed methods are highly efficient for the determination of Cd (II) in real samples.

The performance of the developed methods was further compared to other reports using carbon nanodots (CDs) as a fluorescent probe for Cd (II) determination (Table S3). The results reveal that the developed methods have several advantages including low LODs, wide linearity range, rapid detection, and high selectivity toward Cd (II). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report for the use of a developed CDs sensor on a paper-based system for Cd (II) determination. The higher performance of the developed paper-based system is attributable to the effect of a large number of N-GQDs used in this system. Specifically, a high surface to volume ratio of N-GQDs would lead to a lower LOD. These results were confirmed by a high tolerance ratio on the paper-based sensor (Table 1).

According to previous reports, high selectivity could also arise from the fact that the cellulose-based (i.e., paper-based) system was a good matrix that can be resistant to the influence of other ion suppression. This result can be explained by the stabilization of N-GQDs inside the cellulose matrix instead of being dispersed in the bulk aqueous environment. This occurrence helps to reduce the formation of precipitates between the N-GQD surface and other ions in the paper-based system [32,33]. Therefore, the paper-based sensor developed in this work is a promising method for the practical analysis of Cd (II) in water and herbal medicines with trace levels of Cd (II) contamination.

A novel fluorescent sensor for Cd (II) determination in both solution-based and paper-based systems using N-GQD was proposed. The detection technique employs the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs by Cd (II), which was proven to occur via CHEF mechanism. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was found to linearly increase with increasing Cd (II) concentrations in the range of 1.0–25.0 μg L−1 (LOD = 1.09 μg L−1) and 1.0–15.0 μg L−1 (LOD = 0.59 μg L−1) for the solution-based sensor and the paper-based systems, respectively. The linearity allows the quantitative analysis of Cd (II) in real samples. The proposed method exhibits good selectivity towards Cd (II) over other common interference ions explained. In addition, the advantages of the paper-based sensor also include ease of operation, short analysis time of less than 5 min and low consumption of reagents and samples of less than 5 μL. Therefore, the novel sensor serves as a highly promising tool for the determination of Cd (II) in pharmaceutical and environmental samples with trace levels of Cd (II) contamination.

Puttaraksa Naksen and Siwaluck Boonruang: Methodology, Investigation, Validation and Writing-Original Draft. Nunthawan Yuenyong: Methodology, Validation. Hooi Ling Lee: Supervision, Resources, Review & Editing. Pravena Ramanchandran: Resources Wipark Anutrasakda: Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing, Maliwan Amatatongchai‬‬ and Somkid Pencharee: Review & Editing, Purim Jarujamrus: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, and Writing – Review & Editing.‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are gratefully acknowledged. This work is supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (IRN/502/2563) chaired by Assoc. Prof. Dr Duangjai Nacapricha. The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation is gratefully acknowledged. Prof. Dr. Vinich Promarak, Vidyasirimedhi institute of science and technology (VISTEC) are gratefully also acknowledged for the fluorescence lifetime characterization. The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are also gratefully acknowledged. Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) given to Puttaraksa Naksen is also gratefully acknowledged.
